text: I'm a writer as well, so I'll start off by saying I feel for the guy. It sucks to lose opportunities and money to a machine. I do also want to know what the content was of these blogs, though. If you're pitching original ideas, finding unique angles, and including exclusive quotes from sources authoritative sources, you will not be replaced by AI as a writer so long as the publication/site wants to maintain that quality of product, at least for now. Who knows, there might be an AI in the future that can do all that. But as for the here and now, I think it's important to know what the content was that he was writing. If it's not what I mentioned before and is instead listicles or essentially PR copy you can find on almost any other blog, then I get how the AI replaced him. Again, I feel for the guy. text: I think soon human creativity will become a premium, whether it's art or just human written blogposts. Automation has decimated human-made industries for decades. eg: shoes, cookware, clothes and any other commodity are no longer made in a small town by a family generational business, they're produced in factories on mass, with mostly machines and few humans overseeing, and the items that are still hand made are now expensive, treasured and luxurious, the fact that they're hand made by people and not manufactured by machines is a bonus to a lot of people. But having said that, who are we to now say that automation is bad because it effects the arts instead of manual labour? Was it bad then too, or just bad now? "AI Is bad!" seems like the rallying cry of a class division between those who sit and create with their mind and those who create with their hands - some people think the former is superior to the latter. "AI" is here to stay, and a lot of people don't like that. My suggestion would be to adapt, and to create on platforms that publicly promote that they do not use AI and everything you're reading is written by an actual person with thoughts and feelings on the matter. Good luck.

text:
In the depths where shadows coil,

Demons hiss at the human soul—

That restless spark, that sacred fire

To shape the world with hand and lyre.


They see the painter’s trembling hand,

The poet’s words like grains of sand,

The music rising, pure and bright—

Each note a star that wounds their night.


In secret councils they conspire,

To forge cold tools of rust and wire,

Machines that hum with hollow breath,

Designed to bleed the arts to death.


They twist the worth of brush and pen,

Reduce the master’s work to “ten,”

Mock every canvas, crush each song,

Until the masses sing along.


For every stroke and crafted tale

Reminds them of their ancient jail,

The Creator’s image stamped in clay

That no dark force can wash away.


So they build and grind and sneer and jeer,

To choke the dream, to kill the seer—

But still one spark will slip the chain,

And again consumed by flame…as demons cheer in their pain.

text:
If there is a universal basic income, this income level can eventually become the new poverty line because people have different lives and therefore different consumer habits. Companies and authorities can also use that as a reason to increase prices especially on utilities. I won't be surprised if most of the basic income money will eventually enter the pocket of the rich monopolies.

"The problem is the reliance on having a job to exist in society" - I totally agree with this. Everything cost money - food, water, housing, retirement, medical treatments... 

- AI can greatly increase the financial independance of those who cannot work (e.g. severely paralysed) working by proxy for them but because there's not much money to be made there compered to other areas I doubt there'd be much progress in that area.

- If AI eventually gains a physical body, getting AI to work by proxy earning money for people can be an idea, but that'd open a new cans of worms such as who would pay for the making, delivery, maintenance and development of these robots.

- I've heard local ministers from my country getting people to all study coding, that AI will open new industries (e.g. maintaining AI) but did not discuss what would happen when AI reaches a stage where it could develop and maintain itself.

- Ultimately, what will happen when AI eventually gains enough sentience and start demanding rights. I know whis sounds kinda ridiculous, but at the rate that AI is developing (developers are just developing them without thinking through the consequences apart from money), it could happen.

text:
Hope you enjoyed the video! Here is the writing prompt I used in the video.

⬇ Prompt used in the video ⬇
When writing, follow these writing guidelines:

- Don't blab.
- Keep sentences brief and clear. Limit to 10-20 words.
- Use everyday words that are easy to understand.
- Pick common words over complex ones. Use technical terms only when needed.
- Avoid words with 4+ syllables. If you must use them, keep surrounding text simple.
- Write for a 8th grade reading level.
- Skip overused business terms and jargon like: delve, digital age, cutting-edge, leverage, proactive, pivotal, seamless, fast-paced, game-changer, quest, realm, landscape, evolve, resilient, thrill, unravel, embark, world.
- Make direct statements without hedging.
- Connect ideas naturally without forced transitions.
- Use standard punctuation.
- Vary sentence structure and punctuation naturally.
- Never use: indeed, furthermore, thus, moreover, notwithstanding, ostensibly, consequently, specifically, notably, alternatively.

text:
Serious Q: are people actually too stupid to think of this on their own. I just assumed all this on my very first day using chatGPT like 2 years ago. Here's a REAL GPT tip from my personal prompt arsenal... 
"Okay, remember to think like a clever marketer and keep it somewhat professional but also creative and bold. We want to stand out from our competitors like [xxxx] and this is a critical piece of that puzzle. Another thing to remember: We can't just recycle the same material and catch-phrases and buzzwords over and over again, everything needs to be unique and fresh each iteration. If you need, feel free to search online about [ whatever you need ] Don't forget to think like a clever marketer. Be bold. Be Daring. Be different. 
***Don’t hesitate to try things that are just-so-crazy-they-might-work! Get really creative and think outside your normal box of overused marketing terms please." 

The second-to-last sentence is the real magic!!!!! It'll change your life...and you're welcome!

And if your prompts are not like 800+ words, are you really even trying??? (lots of copy-paste 😉)

text:
my predictions for what will emerge:
The "Human Authenticity" Premium & The Rise of "Artisanal" Marketing:
New Roles: "Brand Authenticity Guardians," storytellers who can weave genuinely personal narratives.

The AI "Orchestrator" or "Prompter-Strategist":
.New Skills: Deep understanding of AI capabilities, creative prompting, iterative refinement, data analysis to optimize AI performance.

Hyper-Personalization Becomes the Default, Demanding Deeper Strategy:
New Focus: Deep customer journey mapping, psychological profiling (ethically), advanced segmentation strategy.

The "Post-Content" Experience Economy:
New Focus: Community management, experiential marketing, interactive content formats (beyond text).


Niche and Boutique Agencies Focusing on "Anti-AI" Strengths:
Prediction: While large agencies adopt AI for scale and efficiency, smaller, boutique agencies will thrive by specializing in areas where AI is weak: deep industry-specific nuance, highly original conceptual work, complex emotional storytelling, and building genuine C-suite level trust.
Emergence: These agencies will emphasize their human talent, unique methodologies, and ability to produce work that is demonstrably impossible for an AI to generate.
